issues.title: East-West BRT (Route A) — Now Operational
issues.governing_body: Madison Common Council / Board of Public Works
issues.summary:
UPDATE (May 8, 2026): Madison's East-West Bus Rapid Transit (BRT) line (Rapid Route A) is nearing completion — the line is entering the homestretch with service expected by summer 2026. Recent Legistar filings show multiple change orders: $383,657 for the main BRT body (Contract 8716, Zenith Tech Inc.) and additional work on the Mineral Point Road sidewalk widening for the BRT corridor (Contract 8717, Parisi Construction). The route runs along East Washington Avenue through the city center and UW-Madison campus to West Towne Mall.

The North-South BRT line (Rapid Route B) has secured federal funding covering 79% of its capital budget ($110 million federal grant announced under the Biden-Harris administration). Route B will have 24 stations and two charging terminals stretching approximately 12.5-15 miles from Kennedy Road on the North Side into Fitchburg. Construction is expected to begin in 2026, with service starting in 2028. This represents a major expansion of Madison's transit infrastructure — two BRT lines creating a cross-shaped rapid transit network that will reshape commuting patterns and create transit-oriented development opportunities along both corridors.

**May 2026 update:** Metro Rapid Route A is now in service, running east/west from Sun Prairie Park and Ride to Junction Rd with 15-minute service Mon-Fri until 8pm and until 7pm on Saturdays. The 62 new electric buses are 20 feet longer and hold 50%+ more passengers than local buses, with built-in heaters, dedicated lanes, bike racks, and ticket machines. Travel times are projected to decrease 20-25%. Design for the North-South Route B continues through 2026 with construction in the 2027-2028 timeframe and service opening in 2028. Federal funding remains in the pipeline per FTA.

public_comment.instructions: Construction is underway. Monitor Board of Public Works agendas for progress updates and change orders.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Madison's BRT system is becoming the city's biggest transit investment — now with TWO lines funded and moving forward. The East-West line is nearing completion, and the North-South line has secured 79% federal funding. Together they'll create a cross-shaped rapid transit network that reshapes commuting patterns and creates transit-oriented development opportunities along both corridors.

issues.title: 2026 Violence Prevention Grant Program — Funding Decisions
issues.governing_body: Board of Health for Madison and Dane County
issues.summary: The Board of Health is allocating funds from the 2026 Violence Prevention Grant Program. Two specific items are in committee: (1) funding to selected applicants from the RFP process, and (2) an agreement with Focused Interruption for hospital-based violence intervention and community response. A presentation on the grant recommendations was given March 25. Public comment is scheduled for April 7.

public_comment.instructions: Public comment at the Board of Health meeting on April 7, 2026.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Violence prevention funding reflects Madison's approach to public safety — investing in intervention and community-based programs alongside traditional policing. The hospital-based violence intervention model (Focused Interruption) is an evidence-based approach that addresses the root causes of violence.

issues.title: Imagination Center at Reindahl Park — Construction Updates
issues.governing_body: Madison Public Library Board / Board of Public Works
issues.summary: The Imagination Center at Reindahl Park (a new library/community center on Madison's east side) continues construction. A $38,849 change order was approved for CCI Corporate Contractors. The Board of Public Works also awarded a fiber connection contract for the facility. The Library Board has ongoing updates on the project. This is a significant community investment in an underserved area of the city.

public_comment.instructions: Monitor Library Board and Board of Public Works agendas for construction updates.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: The Imagination Center is a major public investment in community infrastructure on Madison's east side — the kind of civic investment that strengthens neighborhoods. The Library Board's 2027 Capital Budget Request (also under review) will shape future library investments.

issues.title: Multiple New Housing Subdivisions Advancing — Hill Valley, Starkweather, Midpoint Meadows
issues.governing_body: Board of Public Works / Plan Commission
issues.summary:
UPDATE (May 8, 2026): Multiple housing developments continue advancing. Three subdivisions were approved by the Board of Public Works on April 21: Hill Valley Phase 3 (District 1), Starkweather Plat, and Midpoint Meadows Phase 3. Additionally, three infill development projects have been approved: 2126 Fordem Avenue, 2150 Commercial Avenue, and 5044 Sheboygan Avenue.

⚡ MISSING MIDDLE ZONING OVERHAUL (July 2025): The Common Council voted unanimously to approve zoning reform proposals creating more housing options. Key changes: eliminated minimum private backyard space requirements for housing developments, removed the two-bedroom limit on ADUs, increased maximum ADU size to 1,000 sq ft, and expanded ADU eligibility to lots with up to eight-unit buildings. The city projects population growth of 18,000+ in the next five years — these changes directly address the housing demand.

BROADER HOUSING PROGRESS: The Common Council approved more than $6 million in February 2026 for affordable homeownership programs — creating 29 new affordable owner-occupied homes and funding 5 homeownership service programs. The city also allocated nearly $14 million from the Affordable Housing Fund for new rental developments, including a new 'cottage courts' housing type — collections of up to eight homes arranged around a shared courtyard on a single lot.

PD ZONING UPDATE: The city is moving forward with replacing single/two-family Planned Development (PD) zoning with conventional zoning districts. Public outreach is underway April through June 2026, including informational materials, public information meetings, and input opportunities.

**May 2026 update:** Madison property assessments increased 6.1% for 2026 overall (commercial up 8.3%, residential up 4.8%). The average single-family home value is now $500,300 (3.9% increase from 2025), underscoring the continued importance of housing supply expansion.

public_comment.instructions: Board of Public Works meets April 21. Plan Commission public hearings are listed on madison.legistar.com.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Madison's housing pipeline is active with multiple subdivisions and infill projects moving forward. These developments contribute to the city's housing supply, though individually they are routine approval items.

issues.title: Planning Division Annual Report & 2026 Work Plan Highlights
issues.summary: The Plan Commission received a presentation on the Planning Division's 2026 work plan and annual report. This document outlines the city's development priorities, zoning initiatives, and comprehensive plan implementation strategy for the coming year. It signals what housing and land use policies Madison will prioritize in 2026.

public_comment.instructions: Review the Planning Division annual report on madison.legistar.com for policy direction.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: The work plan reveals Madison's planning priorities for 2026 — worth reviewing to understand the city's direction on housing, zoning, and development policy.

issues.title: Madison Public Library 2027 Capital Budget Request
issues.summary: The Library Board is reviewing the 2027 Capital Budget Request, which will shape library infrastructure investment for the coming year. Also under review: 2025 carry-forward funds and February 2026 financial reports. The library system is one of Madison's most-used public services.

public_comment.instructions: Library Board meetings are open to public comment. Check madison.legistar.com for schedule.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Library infrastructure is civic infrastructure. The 2027 budget request will determine investment in Madison's library system — a high-salience issue given your education focus.

issues.title: Residential Zoning Variances — Keyes Ave & Lake Mendota Drive
issues.summary: Two residential zoning variance requests are before the Zoning Board of Appeals: (1) a front yard setback variance for a detached garage at 2314 Keyes Ave, and (2) a height variance for an addition at 5125 Lake Mendota Drive. The ZBA meets April 16 with public comment available.

public_comment.instructions: Zoning Board of Appeals meeting April 16. Public comment available.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Routine residential variance requests. Low salience unless you are interested in how Madison handles individual property-level zoning decisions.

issues.title: 2026 Infrastructure Investments — Roads, Water, Parks
issues.summary: The Board of Public Works is advancing a large batch of infrastructure projects for April 21 approval: Resurfacing Assessment District (Districts 15, 19), Midvale Boulevard patching (Districts 5, 11), Badger Lane Lift Station replacement, Pressure Zone 4 Interstate Crossing (District 16), Unit Well 12 upgrade (District 11), Manitou Way Storm Box replacement (District 10), Yahara Hills Golf Course Well Transfer Pipe (District 16), Warner Park Pickleball Courts and Worthington Park Futsal Court (Districts 12, 15, 18), and 2026 Parking Garage Maintenance (Districts 2, 4). The 2025 Annual Report for the Board of Public Works is also on the agenda.

public_comment.instructions: Board of Public Works meets April 21. Traffic signal installation at Milwaukee Street & Sprecher Road has a council public hearing.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Routine but important infrastructure maintenance across Madison. The traffic signal installation at Milwaukee & Sprecher has a full council public hearing — unusual for a traffic signal, suggesting community interest.

issues.title: NEW: Southeast Area Plan — Introduced to Common Council, Major Housing & Transit Corridor
issues.summary:
⚡ NEW (May 5, 2026): The Southeast Area Plan was introduced to the Common Council on May 5. The Planning Division submitted the Final Draft Plan for review and adoption.

KEY HOUSING & DEVELOPMENT RECOMMENDATIONS:
• More housing and mixed-use development along major corridors where transit and infrastructure already exist (Milwaukee Street, Cottage Grove Road, US 51-Stoughton Road)
• Redevelopment opportunities including the former American Family East Regional site
• Residents expressed desire for more affordable housing options and more housing types
• The plan aligns with the city's missing middle zoning reforms

PROCESS:
• May 5: Introduced to Common Council
• May-June: Referred to relevant Boards, Commissions and Committees for feedback
• Committees may propose changes before final council decision
• ⚡ COUNCIL ADOPTION VOTE: June 23 (tentative) — 46 DAYS AWAY
• Each committee meeting will include a public hearing

The Southeast Area is one of Madison's key growth corridors — the plan's recommendations will shape housing density, transit connections, and commercial development for the next decade.

**May 5 update:** The Southeast Area Plan Final Draft was submitted for review and introduced to the Common Council at its May 5 meeting. The plan is now in its final adoption phase, with the council vote still scheduled for June 23.

public_comment.instructions: Multiple committee hearings scheduled May-June 2026. Each includes public comment opportunity. Monitor madison.legistar.com for specific dates. Final council vote expected June 23.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: The Southeast Area Plan is a major opportunity to shape Madison's growth pattern. The emphasis on concentrating housing and mixed-use along existing transit corridors aligns with urbanist principles — build where infrastructure already exists rather than sprawling outward. The committee hearing process (May-June) is the window for public input. The June 23 council vote will determine whether the plan's pro-density recommendations survive intact.

issues.title: NEW: Southwest Area Plan — Also Submitted for Final Review
issues.summary:
⚡ NEW (May 5, 2026): The Southwest Area Plan was also submitted alongside the Southeast Area Plan for final review and adoption. Both plans were introduced to Common Council on May 5.

The Southwest Area Plan covers Madison's western growth corridor and includes recommendations for housing development, commercial investment, and transportation infrastructure. Together, the two area plans represent a comprehensive planning update for Madison's southern growth areas.

Both plans will go through the same committee review process with public hearings before returning to Common Council for adoption, tentatively scheduled for June 23.

**May 5 update:** The Southwest Area Plan Final Draft was also submitted alongside the Southeast plan and introduced to Council on May 5. Both plans are now in the final review and adoption phase, with the council vote scheduled for June 23.

public_comment.instructions: Committee hearings scheduled May-June 2026. Monitor madison.legistar.com for dates. Final council vote expected June 23.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: The Southwest Area Plan complements the Southeast Plan — together they shape growth for a significant portion of Madison. The committee review process is the opportunity for input. Worth monitoring alongside the Southeast Plan.

issues.title: Safe Streets and Roads for All Grant Application
issues.summary: The Common Council authorized an application to the U.S. Department of Transportation for a Safe Streets and Roads for All (SS4A) grant for traffic calming and pedestrian safety improvements. The resolution was considered at the May 11 virtual meeting.
issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Federal infrastructure grants can fund pedestrian safety improvements and traffic calming measures that make neighborhoods more walkable and bike-friendly, supporting Madison's broader transportation goals.

issues.title: Dane County Permanent Men's Shelter — $1.148M Funding
issues.summary: Council authorized an Intergovernmental Agreement with Dane County allocating $1,148,000 in county funds for operations at the permanent men's shelter. This is one-time money from the County for 2026 operations.
issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Sustained funding for homeless shelters is critical for addressing Madison's homelessness challenges, and the intergovernmental partnership with Dane County demonstrates collaborative approaches to this regional issue.
